verses.text: Rehoboam goes to Shechem, and the people ask him to lighten the heavy yoke Solomon laid on them, promising loyalty in return. Their request is fair. The whole future of the kingdom hangs on how a new king answers a reasonable plea for mercy.

Rehoboam goes to Shechem, and the people ask him to lighten the heavy yoke Solomon laid on them, promising loyalty in return. Their request is fair. The whole future of the kingdom hangs on how a new king answers a reasonable plea for mercy.

The old men who served Solomon counsel kindness: speak good words, serve the people, and they will be his servants for ever. Seasoned wisdom knows that gentleness wins lasting loyalty. A leader who serves his people will be served by them.

The old men who served Solomon counsel kindness: speak good words, serve the people, and they will be his servants for ever. Seasoned wisdom knows that gentleness wins lasting loyalty. A leader who serves his people will be served by them.

But Rehoboam forsakes their counsel for his young friends, who urge him to answer with threats: 'my little finger shall be thicker than my father's loins.' Pride craves to dominate. Surrounding ourselves only with voices that flatter us is the road to ruin.

Rehoboam answers the people roughly, as the young men advised — 'for the cause was from the LORD,' that His word by Ahijah might stand. Human folly and divine purpose meet: Rehoboam freely sinned, yet God overruled it to fulfill His foretold judgment.

Israel answers, 'What portion have we in David?' and goes home; only Judah stays. Ten tribes are lost in a day by a few proud words. What gentleness might have held for ever, harshness tore apart in an hour.

Israel answers, 'What portion have we in David?' and goes home; only Judah stays. Ten tribes are lost in a day by a few proud words. What gentleness might have held for ever, harshness tore apart in an hour.

When Rehoboam sends his taskmaster, Israel stones him to death, and the king flees to Jerusalem. The rebellion hardens 'unto this day.' Folly not only loses loyalty but provokes violence; the breach, once opened, would not be healed.

When Rehoboam sends his taskmaster, Israel stones him to death, and the king flees to Jerusalem. The rebellion hardens 'unto this day.' Folly not only loses loyalty but provokes violence; the breach, once opened, would not be healed.

Israel makes Jeroboam king, and Rehoboam musters to fight — but God forbids it through Shemaiah: 'this thing is from me.' They obey and disband. Sometimes God's people must accept a painful providence rather than war against what He has plainly ordained.

Jeroboam, fearing the people's hearts will return to David if they keep worshiping at Jerusalem, schemes to keep them home. He trusts his own cunning over God's promise to establish him. Fear that will not rest in God's word breeds its own destruction.

Jeroboam, fearing the people's hearts will return to David if they keep worshiping at Jerusalem, schemes to keep them home. He trusts his own cunning over God's promise to establish him. Fear that will not rest in God's word breeds its own destruction.

So he makes two golden calves and tells Israel, 'these be thy gods... which brought thee up out of Egypt.' To secure his throne he invents a false worship — and it becomes the sin that defines and dooms his dynasty. Idolatry for convenience is idolatry still.

Jeroboam makes shrines, ordains priests not of Levi, and devises a feast of his own choosing. He reshapes God's worship to suit himself. When men make religion serve their own ends, they corrupt the very thing they claim to honour.
